blocks.statement: I can identify a parallel circuit, compute total resistance using the reciprocal rule, compute the SHARED voltage and the CURRENT IN EACH BRANCH, and explain why one branch can fail without breaking the others.

blocks.whatWeKnow: From Day 7: series — one path, shared current.
blocks.connection: Today: multiple paths, shared voltage. Real-world appliances are almost always wired in parallel (so your toaster doesn't die when the microwave is unplugged).

blocks.markdown:
## Many paths, one voltage

A **parallel circuit** flips every series rule. Instead of one loop, the current has multiple independent branches between the same two nodes. The headline rule:

**The SAME voltage is across every branch.**

Each branch then draws its own current by Ohm's law — **I_i = V / R_i** — and the branch currents ADD to give the total leaving the battery. Lower-R branches hog more current (a **current divider**).

Combined resistance uses the reciprocal rule:

**1/R_parallel = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃ + ...**

The result is always SMALLER than the smallest branch — more paths means easier flow. Handy special case: two equal resistors in parallel give R/2.

Why your house is wired this way: every outlet gets the full 120V regardless of what's plugged in elsewhere, and one dead lamp doesn't kill the refrigerator. Each branch lives independently — the opposite of series' break-one-lose-all.

terms.definition: Multiple independent paths between two nodes. The SAME voltage is across each branch. Your house's wall outlets are in parallel — each receives 120V regardless of what's plugged into the others.
terms.cognate: Sp. circuito en paralelo · Pt. circuito em paralelo · HC sikui paralèl

terms.definition: 1/R_parallel = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃ + ... Always SMALLER than the smallest individual R. Two equal resistors in parallel: R_p = R/2. Example: 3Ω || 6Ω: 1/R = 1/3 + 1/6 = 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2 → R = 2Ω. Smaller than either! Adding MORE branches LOWERS total R (more paths for current).
terms.cognate: Sp. resistencia en paralelo · Pt. resistência em paralelo · HC rezistans paralèl

terms.definition: A parallel circuit divides current among branches inversely with resistance. Lower R = more current; higher R = less current. 9V across 3Ω || 6Ω: each branch sees 9V. I₁ = 9/3 = 3 A; I₂ = 9/6 = 1.5 A. Total I_in = 4.5 A.
terms.cognate: Sp. divisor de corriente · Pt. divisor de corrente · HC divizè kouran

blocks.title: Parallel divides CURRENT, not voltage — and a short steals everything
blocks.markdown: In PARALLEL, the SAME voltage is across each branch; it's the CURRENT that divides (more through lower-R branches). In the limit of R → 0 (a short circuit), ALL the current goes through that branch — burning out the wire.

blocks.instruction: Draw three resistors in parallel across a battery. Each branch sees the full battery voltage. Mark currents in each branch. Verify I_total = I₁ + I₂ + I₃.
prompts: Battery on the left; three side-by-side branches, each its own resistor.
Branch current arrows — check they add to I_total.

blocks.prompt: THREE RESISTORS IN PARALLEL — 9V battery across R₁ = 3Ω, R₂ = 6Ω, R₃ = 9Ω in parallel. Find: voltage on each, current in each, total current, R_parallel.
blocks.given: V = 9 V · R₁ = 3 Ω · R₂ = 6 Ω · R₃ = 9 Ω (all in parallel)
blocks.equation: V is the SAME on each branch ; I_i = V / R_i ; 1/R_p = Σ(1/R_i)
blocks.answer: Each branch sees 9V. Currents 3 A / 1.5 A / 1.0 A. R_parallel ≈ 1.64 Ω.

blocks.title: What the battery 'sees' — the equivalent loop
blocks.caption: This diagram can only draw ONE loop, so it shows the worked example AFTER reduction: the three parallel branches (3Ω, 6Ω, 9Ω) collapse into a single equivalent resistor R_p ≈ 1.64 Ω. From the battery's point of view the two circuits are identical: same 9 V, same 5.5 A.

blocks.prompt: YOUR TURN — TWO EQUAL RESISTORS IN PARALLEL. Two 10Ω resistors in parallel. What's R_parallel? If 12V is applied, what's the current in each branch and total?
blocks.equationHint: Shortcut for two EQUAL resistors: R_p = R/2. Then each branch: I = V/R. Total = sum of branches.

blocks.title: Why circuit breakers exist
blocks.markdown: A house has many appliances in parallel. The TV doesn't care if the lamp is on. But every parallel branch DRAWS current from the main supply — too many high-draw devices, and the circuit breaker trips (typically at 15 or 20 A) to prevent wire overheating.

blocks.prompt: 12V across 4Ω || 6Ω. Find R_parallel and the current in each branch. Show one line of work per quantity. Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: In a parallel circuit, the VOLTAGE is the same across every ___. The CURRENTS in each branch ADD up to the ___ current. Total resistance is ___ than any individual resistance.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **35.4** (parallel circuits). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
